title: Thankful for Christ
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Thankful for Christ - Pastor Tito Lyro - Colossians 2_4-10.txt
outline: Introduction: Thanksgiving as a biblical holiday disappearing from public life
Central question: Am I thankful in Christ?
Thanksgiving is the result of having received Christ Jesus the Lord (v.6)
Walking in Christ: rooted, built up, established, abounding with thanksgiving (v.7)
Warning against hollow philosophy and worldly tradition (v.8)
The fullness of the Godhead dwells in Christ (v.9)
We are complete in Him who is head over all (v.10)
True thanksgiving flows from knowing Christ as Lord and Savior
summary: A Thanksgiving-adjacent sermon on Colossians 2:4–10, centered on the question 'Am I thankful in Christ?' As the world skips from Halloween to Christmas erasing Thanksgiving, Paul calls believers to a deeper, Christ-rooted thankfulness. True biblical thanksgiving is only possible for those who have received Christ as Lord (v.6). The passage warns against philosophy and worldly tradition (v.8) while affirming that Christ is the fullness of the Godhead bodily (v.9) and that believers are complete in Him (v.10). Genuine gratitude is thus inseparable from genuine faith in Christ.
transcript:
We are returning to First John next Sunday. This morning, for our scripture reading, we turned to Paul's letter to the Colossians, chapter 2, verses 4 through 10.

As we go through this sermon today, I want you to keep playing in your head this one question: Am I thankful in Christ? That is the one assignment for this morning — am I thankful in Christ?

## Introduction: Thanksgiving and the Life of the Christian

Have you noticed how Thanksgiving as a holiday has disappeared from public life in America? You have Halloween decorations in stores until October 31st, and then on November 1st it is Christmas — and there is no more Thanksgiving. It is gone.

Yet Thanksgiving is one of the few public holidays that actually has a biblical basis. We are encouraged to be a thankful people, encouraged by the Bible to take time to thank the Lord.

In the Bible, the life of the Christian is marked by thanks to God. I encourage you to go to BibleGateway.org or an app on your phone and search for words like "thank," "thankful," and "thanksgiving" together, to see the abundance of references to that in the Scriptures.

The main foundation, the main reason for thanksgiving in the Bible, is the Lord Jesus Christ. And we see that here in Colossians 2:6–7, which we will be considering this morning.

## I. Thanksgiving Is the Result of Having Received Christ (v. 6)

As we make our way through these verses, ask yourselves: am I thankful for Christ?

Paul points out in verse 6 that thanksgiving is the result of having received Jesus Christ. A person cannot truly be thankful in the biblical sense of the word unless he or she has come to know Jesus Christ as Lord and Savior. Look at what Paul says:

> *"As you therefore have received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk in Him... abounding in it with thanksgiving."* (Colossians 2:6–7)

So as you receive Jesus Christ, you abound in thanksgiving.

The Colossians had received Jesus Christ. We tend to think about receiving Christ as a subjective thing — something that happens to each individual person. We think of it in terms of "receiving Jesus into our hearts" or "accepting Jesus into our hearts." And we do need to do that. Every person must come to personal faith in the Lord Jesus Christ. No one can ride the coattails of anybody else. So if you have not come to faith in Jesus Christ, today is the day of salvation. Today is the day you meet Jesus Christ right here. You don't have to wait — you commit to Jesus Christ as your Lord and Savior now.

But that is not actually what Paul is talking about in Colossians 2:6. When he talks about the Colossians receiving Christ, he means they received Christ from their apostles — they objectively received the teachings concerning Christ. That is what caused them to be thankful. The whole truth about Christ that they received from their apostles.

God gave this truth to Paul. Paul gave it to Epaphras, the man who founded the church in Colossae. Epaphras passed it on to them. By the time Paul wrote Colossians, he had never met them. He is writing to a church he doesn't know personally — but it belongs to Christ, and because he is an apostle of Christ, he speaks to them.

We can see this is really what Paul is talking about — the objective receiving of the truth concerning Christ — by the way he refers to faith in verse 7. He doesn't say "your faith in general," but "the faith" — an objective, explicitly-taught faith. A creed. A system of doctrines concerning Christ. Much like what Jude said when he wrote that we are to "contend for the faith once delivered to the saints."

And Paul contrasts that teaching concerning Christ with the empty traditions of men in verse 8: be thankful for Christ, not for the empty philosophy of the world that is tempting you to go to something else.

It is interesting that almost every letter in the New Testament has some warning against our drifting away from Christ — which tells us that this is something we are prone to. Not necessarily wandering away from the church or from Christianity entirely, but from the centrality of Christ in everything we do.

So the Colossians received Christ and, because of that, they abounded in thanksgiving. They received the whole truth concerning Christ. Brothers and sisters, is that not what the church is supposed to do? To receive Christ from those who have gone before us, and to make sure those who come after us receive Christ as well.

Turn to Psalm 78 for a moment — we want to look at verses 1 through 7:

> *"Give ear, O my people, to my law; incline your ears to the words of my mouth. I will open my mouth in a parable; I will utter dark sayings of old, which we have heard and known, and our fathers have told us. We will not hide them from their children, telling to the generation to come the praises of the Lord, and His strength and His wonderful works that He has done. For He established a testimony in Jacob, and appointed a law in Israel, which He commanded our fathers, that they should make them known to their children; that the generation to come might know them, the children who would be born, that they may arise and declare them to their children, that they may set their hope in God, and not forget the works of God, but keep His commandments."* (Psalm 78:1–7)

There are about ten references there to receiving and passing on, receiving and passing on. And Paul says to the Colossians: be thankful, because your fathers in the faith have passed on to you Christ and His fullness.

A special note: those of you who grew up in a faithful Christian home in which your parents passed to you the gospel of Jesus Christ — there is no better existence than that. That was not how I grew up. I wish I had had Sunday school with my parents, but that never happened. And I'm so thankful that you, kids who grew up here, didn't have to go through that — that your parents received from the previous generation what Christ gave them, and handed it to you. Don't rebel against that. Don't kick against the goads. Follow what you have been handed. Make it yours, and be thankful that you have received Christ.

## II. Who Is the Christ They Received?

Back in Colossians 2, who was the Christ the Colossians received from their apostles? Look at verse 6 again: "As you therefore have received Christ Jesus the Lord."

Notice that Paul says "Christ Jesus the Lord" — this is a title. He usually says "Jesus Christ," but here it is "Christ Jesus the Lord."

They received Him as Christ — the Messiah, the anointed One. The One who fulfills all the prophecies and requirements of the Old Testament. The One whom Jesus Himself in Luke 24 opened the Old Testament to show completely — the Law, the Prophets, the Psalms — and taught from it all. This is the One they received: the Messiah, the anointed One, the One who fulfills all things.

They received Him as Jesus — the historical Savior. The One who was born in a manger in Bethlehem. The One who grew up in Nazareth, who died outside of Jerusalem, and who came back to life on the third day. That is the One they received.

But they also received Him as Lord. Christ is the Lord — the One who is sovereign over everything. The One who is God Himself in the flesh. The word Lord in Paul's writings is not simply a title of sovereignty; it is a title of divinity. In the Greek translation of the Old Testament that was largely used among the Jewish community and the early Christian community, the word kurios — the word for Lord in the New Testament — is used to translate the name God chose for Himself: Yahweh.

And we see this clearly in Romans 10, where Paul closes Joel chapter 2, which says "whoever calls on the name of the Lord" — the word Lord there in Joel is Yahweh. And Paul says that is Jesus Christ Himself. That is the Lord you call on.

So they received Him as the Christ — the anointed One of God, who fulfills all the Old Testament predictions and prophecies about the taking away of the sins of the world. They received Him as Jesus — the historical man who actually lived on this earth and came back to life. And they received Him as Lord — as God Himself. And because of that, they abounded in thankfulness.

Receiving Jesus means receiving Him in each and all of these ways. A Jesus who is less than the Savior who lived in history — that is not the Jesus of the Bible. A Jesus who is less than the anointed One of God — not the Jesus of the Bible. A Jesus who is less than the divine — that Jesus cannot save you from your sins. This is a package deal. We either receive the whole package or we receive nothing. We don't get to pick and choose which aspects of Jesus we like. It is all or nothing.

## III. Walking in Christ (vv. 6–7)

Because of who Jesus is and because of our faith in Him, we are to live in a certain way that displays that we are abounding in thanksgiving. Look at verse 6 again: "As you therefore have received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk in Him."

The word walk here is not talking about putting one foot in front of the other. It is talking about the way you live. It is the main verb of what Paul is saying in verses 6 and 7. The central idea is that you are going to walk — to live — in a way that displays you are abounding in thanksgiving to the Lord.

Jesus in our lives produces change. That is the point Paul is making. As you have received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk in Him. Live a certain way because Jesus is in you. You received Him. You received all the teachings concerning Jesus. That should cause you to walk in a certain way.

There is no such thing as an untransformed Christian. There is no such thing as a static Christian. True faith will result in obedience. Obedience won't cause true faith, but true faith will result in obedience. There is no such thing as an unchanged Christian — that is not a category the Bible allows. That is what James says in James 2:26: "For as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead also."

If you say that you have received Jesus but there is nothing in your life that shows it, there is no reason for anyone to believe your claims.

In verse 7, Paul explains how this living looks: "Rooted and built up in Him and established in the faith, as you have been taught, abounding in it with thanksgiving."

### Rooted in Christ

Notice the first descriptive word is "rooted." We are rooted in Christ. And this word is written in a grammatical tense that means the action happened prior to the action of the main verb. That is, we were rooted in Christ even before we started to walk in Him. The rooting happened prior to our obedience. It is not our obedience that causes our rooting in Christ — we have been rooted in Him, and because of that we start to walk.

The idea of rooted is the picture of a plant grounded in the soil. We are in Christ, rooted in Him. He is the rich soil that provides growth for the Christian. All the nutrients we need to grow, all the nutrients we need to walk with Him, are provided by Him.

And this rooting is not like the weeds in my yard. Some weeds come out easily — you pull and the whole root comes out, and it's a joyful thing. That is not the rooting Paul is talking about here. He is talking about the dandelion sort of rooting. If you have ever tried to dig out dandelions, the root goes deep and is difficult to remove. In the dry months when everything is brown, guess what is still green? The dandelions. Because their roots go deep for the nutrients.

Even when you remove the top of the dandelion — which may give you some measure of satisfaction for a moment — more leaves will come up in a few days because the root system is so strong. That is how the Christian is rooted in Christ — as the basis for his or her life. No matter what happens to the surface, the heart is grounded, rooted in Christ, where all the nutrients and life come from.

### Built Up in Christ

Next, Paul uses a construction term: "built up in Him." This expresses the idea of building that is anchored in its foundation. The foundation has been laid, and everything else is now anchored in that foundation. That house will not move, because the true foundation is Christ Himself.

Paul says in Ephesians 2 that the church is laid on the foundation of the apostles and the prophets, with Christ Himself being the chief cornerstone. Everything is tied together to Christ. And if we build on anything else, we are building on sinking sand — and when the storms of life hit, the house falls.

### Established in the Faith

Paul then moves from gardening and construction to the military world: "established in the faith." This word established carries the image of a soldier standing firm on his feet, ready to fight, unable to be moved because he is established in his position.

By Paul's time, this word had come to mean to increase in inner strength. So the Christian is established in Jesus Christ. Notice that the Christian walks in Christ by being established in the faith — the creed, the faith delivered to the church. Part of walking in Christ is growing in knowledge of the faith. We need to grow in Christ.

Part of abounding in thankfulness is getting to know Him better — knowing His Word, studying Him, growing in Him. It is being teachable in His Word. People often say, "Can you stop the theory and just get to the how-to? Just tell me what to do." Or they are looking for mystical shortcuts to growth in Christ. But the Spirit says that good old Bible study, sitting under sermons, reading good books about the Bible — that is walking in Jesus. That is being established in the faith, ready not to be moved.

### Abounding with Thanksgiving

And lastly, Paul describes the Christian walk as a life of thanksgiving. Look again at verse 7: "Rooted and built up in Him and established in the faith, as you have been taught, abounding with thanksgiving."

Giving thanks is not something we do once a year — it is something we are. A Christian is a thankful person. That is our state of being.

Also notice that the measure of thanksgiving is that we are to abound with it. Paul didn't say to be filled with thanksgiving — because if you say "filled" there is a limit. The word abounding means overflowing. You can never measure that. If a cup keeps overflowing, you don't know how much is in there because it is always going over the top. That is what we are called to be.

Why should you be thankful? Paul is not talking about your circumstances or your blessings in general. He is talking about Christ. That is the only reason he gives. Not how your day is going. Not the blessings or curses going on in your life. Not the people around you. But the fact that you have Christ — that is the basis for thanksgiving. Excludes everything else.

Let me ask you: would a Christian have reason to be truly thankful even if he became very sick and suffered physically for a long time? Would a Christian have reason to be thankful if his wife became very sick, suffered, and died? If his children died? If his children turned away from the Lord? If he lost everything he had? If he was falsely accused and lost his reputation?

Paul's answer to every one of those questions, in Colossians, is yes — because we have Christ. None of those things take away from who Christ is, from who we are in Him, or from the promises of the gospel.

Now, don't get me wrong — we pray that things would change. We labor in tears on our knees that circumstances would change, that the gospel of the Lord would shine with goodness in our lives. But that does not mean we have no eternal reasons to be thankful. Paul himself prayed three times that God would remove the thorn in his flesh. And when the answer was, "My grace is sufficient for you," he responded: "Most gladly I will boast in my infirmities, because when I am weak, then I am strong."

The fruit of thankfulness is invariably contentment, and vice versa — contentment and thankfulness feed on each other. And contentment breeds peace. Is the root of most conflicts in our lives the fact that we are discontent with other people? Is the root issue of our depression the fact that we are discontent with our situation? Could we say the same about our anxieties, our pity parties? Peace and completeness in life come from a contented heart, and a contented heart springs from a thankful heart. And a thankful heart is a heart that has received Christ.

## Conclusion

So let me ask you again: are you thankful for Christ?

We have received Christ. We are rooted, grounded, and established in Him. Therefore, we walk with Him every day, in every situation, with thankful hearts.

This time of year gives us a great opportunity to make — or perhaps renew — a commitment to be thankful, since we have Christ. Do you have Christ? If so, are you thankful for Him?
